exercises.title: Shape Orders with a Two-Stage Match and Project Pipeline
exercises.problemHtml:
An aggregation pipeline is an array of stages, and documents flow through it one stage at a time: the output of stage n is the input of stage n+1. Nothing else is magic. $match is the filter stage — it takes the same query syntax you already use with find — and $project reshapes each surviving document, choosing which fields to keep and letting you build new ones from expressions.

The orders collection is seeded with 20 documents. Build two pipelines:

  • A pipeline that keeps only orders whose status is "shipped", then projects region, total, and a new computed field totalWithVat equal to total multiplied by 1.1, rounded to 2 decimal places with $round. Keep _id and finish with $sort on _id ascending so the order is deterministic.
  • A second pipeline that matches the same shipped orders and ends in { $count: "shippedOrders" }, which collapses the whole stream into a single document holding the count.

Note two things while you work. A computed field in $project is written as fieldName: expression, where an expression references document fields with a dollar-prefixed string such as "$total". And $count is a terminal stage — nothing survives it except the one count document. The scaffold provides both pipeline skeletons.
exercises.inputSpec: An orders collection of 20 documents with fields _id (1..20), customerId, region (north/south/east/west), status (shipped/pending/delivered/cancelled), total, placedAt (dates through 2024), an items array of { sku, qty, price } sub-documents, and an optional coupon field. Order 16 has an empty items array and order 18 has no items field. A customers collection holds c1..c5 with name, tier and city.
exercises.outputSpec: The first pipeline returns 8 documents (_id 1, 4, 7, 10, 12, 14, 17, 18) each with _id, region, total and totalWithVat = total * 1.1. The second returns exactly [ { shippedOrders: 8 } ].
exercises.constraints: Use the aggregation pipeline, not find(). $match must be the first stage. Round totalWithVat with $round to 2 decimals. Do not compute the count in application code — use the $count stage.

examplesJson.input: db.orders.aggregate([{ $match: { status: "shipped" } }, { $project: { region: 1, total: 1, totalWithVat: { $round: [{ $multiply: ["$total", 1.1] }, 2] } } }, { $sort: { _id: 1 } }])
examplesJson.output: [ { _id: 1, region: 'north', total: 90, totalWithVat: 99 }, { _id: 4, region: 'east', total: 130, totalWithVat: 143 }, { _id: 7, region: 'north', total: 110, totalWithVat: 121 }, { _id: 10, region: 'west', total: 60, totalWithVat: 66 }, { _id: 12, region: 'south', total: 120, totalWithVat: 132 }, { _id: 14, region: 'north', total: 320, totalWithVat: 352 }, { _id: 17, region: 'south', total: 150, totalWithVat: 165 }, { _id: 18, region: 'east', total: 0, totalWithVat: 0 } ]
examplesJson.explanation: Eight of the twenty orders are shipped. $project keeps region and total, drops every other field except _id, and adds totalWithVat from the expression.

examplesJson.input: db.orders.aggregate([{ $match: { status: "shipped" } }, { $count: "shippedOrders" }])
examplesJson.output: [ { shippedOrders: 8 } ]
examplesJson.explanation: $count consumes the 8 documents that reached it and emits one document whose single field is named by the string you passed.

hintsJson: A pipeline is an array; each element is one stage object, and documents flow left to right.
$match takes exactly the filter document you would pass to find, so { status: "shipped" } is the whole stage.
In $project, 1 keeps a field and fieldName: expression creates one; reference a field inside an expression as "$total".
$round takes an array: { $round: [{ $multiply: ["$total", 1.1] }, 2] }. Finish the counting pipeline with { $count: "shippedOrders" }.

starterCodeJson.name: pipeline.js

solutionCodeJson.name: pipeline.js

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The pipeline is a conveyor belt. Twenty documents enter $match, eight survive it, and those eight are what $project sees — the later stages never know the other twelve existed. That mental model is the whole framework: every stage transforms a stream of documents into another stream, and stages can add, remove, reorder, split or collapse documents.

$project does two jobs at once here. Listing region: 1 and total: 1 is inclusion, exactly like a find projection, and everything not listed is dropped — except _id, which is included by default and would need _id: 0 to remove. The third entry, totalWithVat, is an expression: { $multiply: ["$total", 1.1] } reads the current document's total (the dollar prefix means "value of this field") and multiplies it, then $round trims the floating-point tail. Without $round, 110 * 1.1 prints as 121.00000000000001 — binary floating point, not a MongoDB quirk — which is why money calculations round explicitly.

The final $sort matters more than it looks. Aggregation makes no ordering promise unless you ask for one, so a result that "happens" to come back in _id order today can come back differently once the collection grows or the plan changes. The second pipeline shows the other kind of stage: $count is a collapsing terminal stage that turns the entire remaining stream into one document, so nothing can follow it usefully.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[orders collection 20 docs] --> B[stage 1 match status shipped]
  B --> C[8 docs]
  C --> D[stage 2 project region total totalWithVat]
  D --> E[8 reshaped docs]
  E --> F[stage 3 sort by id ascending]
  F --> G[final result]

exercises.title: Aggregate Revenue per Region with $group Accumulators
exercises.problemHtml:
$group is where aggregation stops looking like a fancy find. It partitions the incoming stream by a group key you place in _id, then folds every document in each partition into one output document using accumulators. The output _id is the key itself; every other output field must be produced by an accumulator such as $sum, $avg, $min or $max.

Produce a per-region sales summary from the orders collection:

  • First drop cancelled orders with $match — they are not revenue.
  • $group by "$region", producing orders as a document count with { $sum: 1 }, revenue as { $sum: "$total" }, avgTotal as { $avg: "$total" }, and maxTotal as { $max: "$total" }.
  • Follow with a $project that rounds avgTotal to 2 decimals and keeps the other three fields.
  • $sort by revenue descending so the strongest region is first.

The idiom { $sum: 1 } is worth memorising: it adds the literal 1 for every document in the group, which is how you count rows. Rounding has to happen after the group because $round is an expression, not an accumulator, and cannot be nested directly around $avg inside $group. The scaffold provides the stage skeleton.
exercises.inputSpec: An orders collection of 20 documents with fields _id (1..20), customerId, region (north/south/east/west), status (shipped/pending/delivered/cancelled), total, placedAt (dates through 2024), an items array of { sku, qty, price } sub-documents, and an optional coupon field. Order 16 has an empty items array and order 18 has no items field. A customers collection holds c1..c5 with name, tier and city.
exercises.outputSpec: Four documents ordered by revenue descending: north (orders 8, revenue 980, maxTotal 320, avgTotal 122.5), south (4, 450, 150, 112.5), east (3, 290, 160, 96.67), west (3, 230, 100, 76.67).
exercises.constraints: Exclude cancelled orders before grouping. Count with { $sum: 1 }, not by pushing documents and taking a size. Round avgTotal in a stage after $group. Sort by revenue descending.

examplesJson.input: db.orders.aggregate([{ $match: { status: { $ne: "cancelled" } } }, { $group: { _id: "$region", orders: { $sum: 1 }, revenue: { $sum: "$total" }, avgTotal: { $avg: "$total" }, maxTotal: { $max: "$total" } } }, { $project: { orders: 1, revenue: 1, avgTotal: { $round: ["$avgTotal", 2] }, maxTotal: 1 } }, { $sort: { revenue: -1 } }])
examplesJson.output: [ { _id: 'north', orders: 8, revenue: 980, maxTotal: 320, avgTotal: 122.5 }, { _id: 'south', orders: 4, revenue: 450, maxTotal: 150, avgTotal: 112.5 }, { _id: 'east', orders: 3, revenue: 290, maxTotal: 160, avgTotal: 96.67 }, { _id: 'west', orders: 3, revenue: 230, maxTotal: 100, avgTotal: 76.67 } ]
examplesJson.explanation: North has 8 non-cancelled orders totalling 980. East and west each lose one cancelled order, so they group only 3 documents each. avgTotal for east is 290/3 = 96.666..., rounded to 96.67.

examplesJson.input: db.orders.aggregate([{ $group: { _id: "$region", orders: { $sum: 1 } } }, { $sort: { _id: 1 } }])
examplesJson.output: [ { _id: 'east', orders: 4 }, { _id: 'north', orders: 8 }, { _id: 'south', orders: 4 }, { _id: 'west', orders: 4 } ]
examplesJson.explanation: Without the $match, the cancelled orders 5 (west) and 13 (east) are included, so east and west report 4 instead of 3 — proof that the filter stage changes what $group sees.

hintsJson: Grouping needs two things: what to group by, and what to compute for each group.
The group key goes in _id: { $group: { _id: "$region", ... } }; every other field needs an accumulator.
Counting documents is { $sum: 1 }; summing a field is { $sum: "$total" }. $avg and $max work the same way.
$round is an expression, so apply it in a $project after the $group: avgTotal: { $round: ["$avgTotal", 2] }.

starterCodeJson.name: pipeline.js

solutionCodeJson.name: pipeline.js

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
$group reads every incoming document, evaluates the _id expression to decide which bucket it belongs to, and feeds it to each accumulator for that bucket. With _id: "$region" the eighteen non-cancelled orders land in four buckets, and each bucket emits exactly one document. The accumulators run in a single pass: $sum: 1 adds one per document (the counting idiom), $sum: "$total" adds the field's value, and $avg and $max track a running mean and maximum. Because it is one pass over the stream, computing five statistics costs no more reads than computing one.

Two ordering decisions carry all the meaning here. Putting $match first removes the cancelled orders before they can pollute the sums — grouping first and filtering afterwards would be both wrong (cancelled money already added) and slower. Putting $round after $group is forced by the type system: accumulators like $avg are only legal directly inside $group, while $round is an ordinary expression that operates on a field that already exists, so it belongs in a later $project or $addFields.

The trap most learners hit is assuming the groups come back in a useful order. They do not — $group emits buckets in an unspecified order (hash order in practice), so the trailing $sort is what makes "north first" a guarantee rather than a coincidence. Note also that the output document keeps the field order MongoDB chooses, which is why maxTotal prints before avgTotal: field order in the printed result is cosmetic and never something to assert on.

exercises.title: Page a Ranked Report with $sort, $limit, $skip and Read the Explain Plan
exercises.problemHtml:
Ranked lists — top sellers, biggest orders, slowest endpoints — are $sort followed by $limit, and the page after that is $skip before the $limit. Inside a pipeline these are real stages, so their position is a performance decision, not a cosmetic one: a $match placed before a $sort shrinks the set that has to be sorted, and a $limit right after a $sort lets the server keep only the top N in memory instead of ordering everything.

Working with delivered orders:

  • Build a pipeline that matches status: "delivered", sorts by total descending with _id ascending as a tie-breaker, limits to 3, and projects region and total.
  • Build the second page: the same pipeline with $skip: 3 before the $limit: 3.
  • Explain the first pipeline with db.orders.explain("queryPlanner").aggregate([...]), capture the result in a variable, and print plan.queryPlanner.winningPlan with printjson.

Read the plan carefully: the $match does not appear as a pipeline stage at all — it was pushed down into the query layer and shows up as a filter on the COLLSCAN, and the $limit was absorbed into the SORT stage as limitAmount. That fusion is exactly the optimisation you are trying to enable. The scaffold provides all three skeletons.
exercises.inputSpec: An orders collection of 20 documents with fields _id (1..20), customerId, region (north/south/east/west), status (shipped/pending/delivered/cancelled), total, placedAt (dates through 2024), an items array of { sku, qty, price } sub-documents, and an optional coupon field. Order 16 has an empty items array and order 18 has no items field. A customers collection holds c1..c5 with name, tier and city. There are 7 delivered orders. No index exists on total, so the plan is a collection scan.
exercises.outputSpec: Page 1 returns [ { _id: 20, region: 'north', total: 220 }, { _id: 11, region: 'north', total: 170 }, { _id: 8, region: 'east', total: 160 } ]; page 2 returns _id 19 (west, 100), 15 (west, 70), 6 (south, 30). The printed winningPlan is a SORT stage with sortPattern { total: -1, _id: 1 }, limitAmount 3, over an inputStage COLLSCAN whose filter is { status: { $eq: 'delivered' } }.
exercises.constraints: Sort with an explicit tie-breaker so paging is stable. $skip must come before $limit. Use explain("queryPlanner") — do not use executionStats, whose timings vary between runs.

examplesJson.input: db.orders.aggregate([{ $match: { status: "delivered" } }, { $sort: { total: -1, _id: 1 } }, { $limit: 3 }, { $project: { region: 1, total: 1 } }])
examplesJson.output: [ { _id: 20, region: 'north', total: 220 }, { _id: 11, region: 'north', total: 170 }, { _id: 8, region: 'east', total: 160 } ]
examplesJson.explanation: The three largest delivered orders, highest first. $limit keeps only the first 3 documents the sort produces.

examplesJson.input: db.orders.aggregate([{ $match: { status: "delivered" } }, { $sort: { total: -1, _id: 1 } }, { $skip: 3 }, { $limit: 3 }, { $project: { region: 1, total: 1 } }])
examplesJson.output: [ { _id: 19, region: 'west', total: 100 }, { _id: 15, region: 'west', total: 70 }, { _id: 6, region: 'south', total: 30 } ]
examplesJson.explanation: Page 2: the same ordering, with the first three discarded by $skip. Orders 20, 11 and 8 are gone and the next three appear.

examplesJson.input: const plan = db.orders.explain("queryPlanner").aggregate([{ $match: { status: "delivered" } }, { $sort: { total: -1, _id: 1 } }, { $limit: 3 }]); printjson(plan.queryPlanner.winningPlan)
examplesJson.output: { stage: 'SORT', sortPattern: { total: -1, _id: 1 }, memLimit: 104857600, limitAmount: 3, type: 'simple', inputStage: { stage: 'COLLSCAN', filter: { status: { '$eq': 'delivered' } }, direction: 'forward' } }
examplesJson.explanation: The $match became the COLLSCAN filter and the $limit became limitAmount on the SORT — the optimiser fused three pipeline stages into one plan tree.

hintsJson: Ordering, paging and inspection are three separate pipelines over the same data.
$sort takes { field: 1 } for ascending and { field: -1 } for descending; list a second field to break ties.
For page N the stages are $sort, then $skip of (N-1)*pageSize, then $limit of pageSize — skip always before limit.
db.orders.explain("queryPlanner").aggregate([...]) returns an object; the plan tree lives at .queryPlanner.winningPlan.

starterCodeJson.name: pipeline.js

solutionCodeJson.name: pipeline.js

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The three stages compose in a fixed logical order: sort establishes a total ordering, skip discards a prefix of it, limit takes the next slice. Writing $limit before $skip would mean "take 3, then throw 3 away" and return nothing, which is the classic paging bug. The tie-breaker matters just as much: orders 2 and 17 both total 150 elsewhere in this dataset, and without a second sort key such ties can be ordered differently between runs, so a document can appear on both page 1 and page 2 or on neither. Sorting on a unique field last — here _id — makes the ordering total and paging stable.

The explain output is the payoff. What you wrote as four stages does not survive as four stages: the optimiser pushed $match down into the query layer, where it appears as filter: { status: { $eq: 'delivered' } } on the COLLSCAN, and folded $limit: 3 into the SORT as limitAmount: 3. That fold is a genuine algorithmic change — a top-3 sort keeps a 3-element heap instead of materialising and ordering all seven documents, and the same trick is what saves a server when the input is a million documents rather than twenty. memLimit: 104857600 is the 100 MB in-memory sort budget; exceed it without a supporting index and the pipeline errors unless you pass allowDiskUse: true.

The rule this exercise is really teaching is: put $match as early as possible so fewer documents reach the expensive stages, and put $limit immediately after $sort so the sort itself can be bounded. The optimiser will move a $match earlier on its own when it provably can, but it cannot do so past stages that invent fields — once you $group or $project a computed value, a later filter on that value has to run after it. Note also that explain("queryPlanner") plans without running; executionStats would execute the pipeline and report timings that differ on every run.

exercises.title: Explode Order Line Items with $unwind and preserveNullAndEmptyArrays
exercises.problemHtml:
$unwind takes a document containing an array and emits one output document per array element, each carrying a single element in place of the array. A collection of 20 orders becomes a stream of individual line items — the shape you need before you can group, sum or rank by anything inside the array. The cost is that your document count changes, and understanding exactly how it changes is what separates a correct report from a plausible-looking wrong one.

Measure the multiplication effect on the orders collection, where order 16 has an empty items array and order 18 has no items field at all:

  • Count the raw documents with db.orders.countDocuments().
  • Pipeline: $unwind: "$items" then { $count: "itemRows" } — how many line items exist.
  • Pipeline: $unwind in its object form with preserveNullAndEmptyArrays: true, then $count into rowsWithEmpties. Compare the two counts and explain the difference.
  • Pipeline: match order 1 only, unwind with includeArrayIndex: "idx", and project idx plus sku and qty lifted out of the unwound element with "$items.sku" and "$items.qty".

Predict all three counts before running anything, then check yourself. The scaffold provides the four skeletons.
exercises.inputSpec: An orders collection of 20 documents with fields _id (1..20), customerId, region (north/south/east/west), status (shipped/pending/delivered/cancelled), total, placedAt (dates through 2024), an items array of { sku, qty, price } sub-documents, and an optional coupon field. Order 16 has an empty items array and order 18 has no items field. A customers collection holds c1..c5 with name, tier and city. The 20 orders hold 27 line items in total; order 16 has items: [] and order 18 has no items field.
exercises.outputSpec: countDocuments() returns 20; the plain $unwind pipeline returns [ { itemRows: 27 } ]; the preserveNullAndEmptyArrays pipeline returns [ { rowsWithEmpties: 29 } ]; the order-1 pipeline returns two documents with idx Long(0) sku KB qty 1 and idx Long(1) sku MS qty 2.
exercises.constraints: Use the $unwind stage — do not flatten arrays in application code. Use the object form { path, preserveNullAndEmptyArrays } and { path, includeArrayIndex } rather than the shorthand where options are needed.

examplesJson.input: db.orders.aggregate([{ $unwind: "$items" }, { $count: "itemRows" }])
examplesJson.output: [ { itemRows: 27 } ]
examplesJson.explanation: 27 line items across the collection. Orders 16 (empty array) and 18 (no items field) produce zero output documents and vanish from the stream.

examplesJson.input: db.orders.aggregate([{ $unwind: { path: "$items", preserveNullAndEmptyArrays: true } }, { $count: "rowsWithEmpties" }])
examplesJson.output: [ { rowsWithEmpties: 29 } ]
examplesJson.explanation: Two more rows than before: orders 16 and 18 now survive, each as one document with no items field set.

examplesJson.input: db.orders.aggregate([{ $match: { _id: 1 } }, { $unwind: { path: "$items", includeArrayIndex: "idx" } }, { $project: { idx: 1, sku: "$items.sku", qty: "$items.qty" } }])
examplesJson.output: [ { _id: 1, idx: Long('0'), sku: 'KB', qty: 1 }, { _id: 1, idx: Long('1'), sku: 'MS', qty: 2 } ]
examplesJson.explanation: One order with two items becomes two documents sharing _id 1; includeArrayIndex records the original position as a 64-bit integer.

hintsJson: One document with an array of n elements becomes n documents — so the total count changes.
The shorthand { $unwind: "$items" } silently drops documents whose array is empty or missing.
The object form { $unwind: { path: "$items", preserveNullAndEmptyArrays: true } } keeps those documents instead.
After unwinding, the element sits at "$items", so project sub-fields as sku: "$items.sku". includeArrayIndex: "idx" adds the position.

starterCodeJson.name: pipeline.js

solutionCodeJson.name: pipeline.js

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
$unwind multiplies documents. An order with three line items leaves the stage as three documents, identical except that items now holds a single sub-document rather than the array. Summing the array lengths across the collection gives 27, which is exactly the itemRows count — every remaining field, including _id, is duplicated onto each row, so _id is no longer unique in the stream.

The 27-versus-29 gap is the point of the exercise. By default $unwind emits nothing for a document whose array is empty, whose field is null, or whose field is missing entirely — from a set-theoretic view it is an inner join against the array, and zero elements means zero rows. Order 16 (items: []) and order 18 (no items field) therefore disappear silently. Setting preserveNullAndEmptyArrays: true turns it into a left outer join: those two orders survive as one document each, with items simply absent, taking the count to 29.

This is the single most common source of quietly wrong aggregation reports. Unwind order line items to compute revenue per customer and every customer whose orders contained no items vanishes from the report — not with a zero, but with no row at all. The fix is either preserveNullAndEmptyArrays: true plus an $ifNull when you read the element, or an explicit acknowledgement that empty orders are out of scope. The other habit worth forming is remembering that after unwinding you address the element as "$items.sku", not "$items.0.sku", and that includeArrayIndex gives you the original position as a 64-bit integer (printed as Long('0') in mongosh) when order within the array carries meaning.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[order 1 with items KB and MS] --> B[unwind items]
  B --> C[doc id 1 items KB]
  B --> D[doc id 1 items MS]
  E[order 16 with empty items array] --> F[unwind items]
  F --> G[no output document by default]
  F --> H[one document when preserveNullAndEmptyArrays is true]

exercises.title: Group by a Compound Key with $push, $addToSet, $first and $last
exercises.problemHtml:
A group key does not have to be a single field. Set _id to a document and you group by the combination — the equivalent of SQL's GROUP BY a, b — and the resulting _id is that whole sub-document. Alongside the numeric accumulators, MongoDB gives you collection accumulators: $push keeps every value, $addToSet keeps the distinct ones, and $first and $last take the value from the first and last document to reach the group.

Build a per-region, per-status breakdown of orders:

  • $sort by placedAt ascending first — $first and $last mean "first and last document that arrived", so they are only meaningful if you impose the order yourself.
  • $group with _id: { region: "$region", status: "$status" } and these fields: orders counting documents, firstOrderId via $first of "$_id", lastOrderId via $last, ids via $push of "$_id", buyers via $addToSet of "$customerId", plus minTotal and maxTotal.
  • $addFields replacing buyers with { $sortArray: { input: "$buyers", sortBy: 1 } }, because $addToSet makes no ordering promise.
  • $sort by "_id.region" then "_id.status" — note the dotted path into the compound key.

Twelve region-and-status combinations exist in the data. The scaffold provides the stage skeleton.
exercises.inputSpec: An orders collection of 20 documents with fields _id (1..20), customerId, region (north/south/east/west), status (shipped/pending/delivered/cancelled), total, placedAt (dates through 2024), an items array of { sku, qty, price } sub-documents, and an optional coupon field. Order 16 has an empty items array and order 18 has no items field. A customers collection holds c1..c5 with name, tier and city.
exercises.outputSpec: Twelve documents ordered by region then status. For example { _id: { region: 'north', status: 'shipped' }, orders: 3, firstOrderId: 1, lastOrderId: 14, ids: [ 1, 7, 14 ], buyers: [ 'c1', 'c5' ], minTotal: 90, maxTotal: 320 } and { _id: { region: 'east', status: 'shipped' }, orders: 2, firstOrderId: 4, lastOrderId: 18, ids: [ 4, 18 ], buyers: [ 'c3' ], minTotal: 0, maxTotal: 130 }.
exercises.constraints: The group key must be a compound document, not a concatenated string. Sort by placedAt before grouping so $first and $last are deterministic. Sort the $addToSet result with $sortArray — do not rely on its natural order.

examplesJson.input: The full pipeline, looking at the north/shipped group
examplesJson.output: { _id: { region: 'north', status: 'shipped' }, orders: 3, firstOrderId: 1, lastOrderId: 14, ids: [ 1, 7, 14 ], buyers: [ 'c1', 'c5' ], minTotal: 90, maxTotal: 320 }
examplesJson.explanation: Orders 1, 7 and 14 are north and shipped. Sorted by placedAt they arrive in that order, so $first is 1 and $last is 14. $push keeps all three ids; $addToSet collapses customers c1, c5, c1 to two distinct values.

examplesJson.input: The same pipeline, looking at the north/delivered group
examplesJson.output: { _id: { region: 'north', status: 'delivered' }, orders: 3, firstOrderId: 3, lastOrderId: 20, ids: [ 3, 11, 20 ], buyers: [ 'c1', 'c5' ], minTotal: 20, maxTotal: 220 }
examplesJson.explanation: A different status in the same region is a different group entirely, because the key is the pair. minTotal and maxTotal span orders 3 (20) and 20 (220).

examplesJson.input: The same pipeline, looking at the west/cancelled group
examplesJson.output: { _id: { region: 'west', status: 'cancelled' }, orders: 1, firstOrderId: 5, lastOrderId: 5, ids: [ 5 ], buyers: [ 'c4' ], minTotal: 300, maxTotal: 300 }
examplesJson.explanation: A single-document group: $first and $last are the same order, $push yields a one-element array, and min equals max.

hintsJson: Grouping by two fields at once means the key itself is a document.
Write _id: { region: "$region", status: "$status" }; the output _id is that whole sub-document.
$push: "$_id" collects every value including duplicates; $addToSet: "$customerId" keeps only distinct ones.
$first and $last depend on arrival order, so $sort by placedAt before $group; and sort the compound key with dotted paths like { "_id.region": 1 }.

starterCodeJson.name: pipeline.js

solutionCodeJson.name: pipeline.js

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
Setting _id to { region: "$region", status: "$status" } makes the group key a pair, so north/shipped and north/delivered are distinct buckets and the twenty orders fall into twelve groups. The key is echoed back as a nested document, which is why the final sort uses dotted paths: { "_id.region": 1, "_id.status": 1 }. Sorting on _id alone would compare whole documents field by field, which happens to work here but breaks as soon as the key's field order changes.

The collection accumulators differ in exactly one way that matters. $push appends every value it sees, so ids is a faithful list of the group's members and its length equals the count. $addToSet deduplicates, so the north/shipped group's customers c1, c5 and c1 collapse to [ 'c1', 'c5' ] — use it for "which distinct entities appear", use $push for "list every row". Both build arrays in memory, and a single group is capped at 100 MB, so pushing whole documents across a large collection is the classic way to blow up a pipeline; push only the fields you need.

$first and $last are the subtle pair. They are not "smallest" and "largest" — they take the value from the first and last document that reached the group, so their meaning is defined entirely by the preceding $sort. With $sort: { placedAt: 1 } they become "earliest order" and "latest order", which is genuinely useful; without it they return whatever the storage engine happened to hand over. For extremes of a value, $min and $max are the correct tools and need no sort at all. The same caution applies to $addToSet, whose output order is unspecified: the $sortArray in the following $addFields is what makes buyers reproducible rather than merely usually-the-same.

exercises.title: Derive Order Flags with $addFields, $set, $cond, $switch and $ifNull
exercises.problemHtml:
$project is destructive: anything you do not list is dropped. When you only want to add a derived field, $addFields (and its identical alias $set) is the right stage — it keeps the whole document and layers new fields on top, or overwrites existing ones with the same name. Combined with the conditional expressions, this is how raw documents grow the flags and bands that a report needs.

Enrich the orders collection, remembering that coupon is missing on most orders, order 16 has an empty items array, and order 18 has no items field at all:

  • $addFields that overwrites coupon with { $ifNull: ["$coupon", "NONE"] } and adds itemCount as { $size: { $ifNull: ["$items", []] } }.
  • $set that adds sizeBand using $switch: "large" when total is at least 200, "medium" when it is at least 100, otherwise "small"; and isCancelled using the array form of $cond comparing status to "cancelled".
  • $match down to _id in [5, 14, 16, 18] — one cancelled order, one couponed order, one with an empty array and one with no array.
  • $project total, coupon, itemCount, sizeBand and isCancelled, then $sort by _id.

Note that the $match deliberately comes after the computed fields — it filters on _id, which existed all along, but filtering on sizeBand would only be legal here. The scaffold provides the stage skeleton.
exercises.inputSpec: An orders collection of 20 documents with fields _id (1..20), customerId, region (north/south/east/west), status (shipped/pending/delivered/cancelled), total, placedAt (dates through 2024), an items array of { sku, qty, price } sub-documents, and an optional coupon field. Order 16 has an empty items array and order 18 has no items field. A customers collection holds c1..c5 with name, tier and city.
exercises.outputSpec: Four documents: _id 5 (total 300, coupon 'NONE', itemCount 1, sizeBand 'large', isCancelled true), _id 14 (320, 'SAVE10', 2, 'large', false), _id 16 (0, 'NONE', 0, 'small', false) and _id 18 (0, 'NONE', 0, 'small', false).
exercises.constraints: Use $addFields/$set to keep the original fields — do not rebuild documents with $project. $size errors on a missing field, so guard it with $ifNull. Use $switch for the three-way band, not nested $cond.

examplesJson.input: The full pipeline, row for order 14 (total 320, coupon SAVE10, two line items)
examplesJson.output: { _id: 14, total: 320, coupon: 'SAVE10', itemCount: 2, sizeBand: 'large', isCancelled: false }
examplesJson.explanation: coupon exists so $ifNull returns it unchanged; $size counts the two line items; 320 is at least 200 so the first $switch branch wins; the status is shipped so $cond yields false.

examplesJson.input: The full pipeline, row for order 18 (no items field, no coupon, total 0)
examplesJson.output: { _id: 18, total: 0, coupon: 'NONE', itemCount: 0, sizeBand: 'small', isCancelled: false }
examplesJson.explanation: $ifNull supplies "NONE" for the missing coupon and an empty array for the missing items, so $size returns 0 instead of raising an error. No $switch branch matches 0, so the default applies.

examplesJson.input: The full pipeline, row for order 5 (cancelled, total 300)
examplesJson.output: { _id: 5, total: 300, coupon: 'NONE', itemCount: 1, sizeBand: 'large', isCancelled: true }
examplesJson.explanation: The $cond comparison is true for the cancelled status. Note $switch evaluates branches top to bottom, so 300 matches the 200 branch and never reaches the 100 branch.

hintsJson: You want the original document plus extra fields, so reach for the additive stage rather than the reshaping one.
$addFields and $set are the same stage under two names; both keep every existing field.
$ifNull: ["$coupon", "NONE"] substitutes a default when the field is missing or null — wrap $size the same way with an empty array.
$switch takes { branches: [ { case, then }, ... ], default }, and branches are tested in order; $cond in array form is [ condition, thenValue, elseValue ].

starterCodeJson.name: pipeline.js

solutionCodeJson.name: pipeline.js

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
$addFields and $set are literally the same stage — $set was added in MongoDB 4.2 as an alias that reads better when you are overwriting rather than adding. Both merge their result into the incoming document, so writing coupon: { $ifNull: ["$coupon", "NONE"] } replaces the field in place while every other field survives untouched. That is the difference from $project, which starts from nothing and keeps only what you list.

$ifNull does the defensive work, and it is doing two different jobs here. On coupon it substitutes a display value so downstream grouping does not need a special case for absent fields. On items it prevents an error: $size throws if its argument is not an array, so order 18, which has no items field at all, would abort the entire pipeline with "The argument to $size must be an array" — one bad document out of twenty kills the whole report. Wrapping it as { $size: { $ifNull: ["$items", []] } } yields 0 for both the missing field and the empty array of order 16. Schema-flexible collections make this pattern routine rather than paranoid.

The two conditionals cover the common shapes. $cond is the ternary: in array form [ condition, thenValue, elseValue ], or in object form with if, then, else keys. $switch is the multi-way version, and its branches are evaluated top to bottom with the first match winning — which is why the 200 branch must be listed before the 100 branch. Reverse them and every order over 200 would be labelled medium, a bug that produces perfectly plausible output. If no branch matches and you omitted default, $switch raises an error rather than returning null, so always supply one. Finally, note that a computed field only exists after the stage that creates it: a $match on sizeBand must sit after this $set, and the optimiser cannot hoist it earlier the way it hoists a match on a stored field.

exercises.title: Bucket Revenue by Month and Quarter with Date Expressions
exercises.problemHtml:
Almost every business report is "something per time period", and the way you express the period is by grouping on a date expression rather than a stored field. Two operators dominate: $dateToString formats a date into a string key such as "2024-03", which sorts lexicographically in chronological order and is ready to display; $dateTrunc rounds a date down to the start of a unit — hour, day, week, month, quarter, year — keeping it a real date so it can still be compared and formatted later.

Report on non-cancelled orders in the orders collection, whose placedAt values span January to July 2024:

  • A monthly pipeline: $match out cancelled orders, then $group with _id: { $dateToString: { format: "%Y-%m", date: "$placedAt" } } computing orders and revenue, then $sort by _id ascending.
  • A quarterly pipeline over the same filtered set, grouping on { $dateTrunc: { date: "$placedAt", unit: "quarter" } } and summing revenue, sorted by _id ascending.

Check the two results against each other: the three quarterly figures must add up to the sum of the seven monthly figures, because they partition the same documents. If they disagree, one of the two pipelines is filtering differently. The scaffold provides both skeletons.
exercises.inputSpec: An orders collection of 20 documents with fields _id (1..20), customerId, region (north/south/east/west), status (shipped/pending/delivered/cancelled), total, placedAt (dates through 2024), an items array of { sku, qty, price } sub-documents, and an optional coupon field. Order 16 has an empty items array and order 18 has no items field. A customers collection holds c1..c5 with name, tier and city. placedAt values run from 2024-01-05 to 2024-07-18; two orders are cancelled and excluded.
exercises.constraints: Exclude cancelled orders in both pipelines. Group on the date expression itself — do not add a month field with $addFields first. Use %Y-%m (zero-padded) so the string keys sort chronologically.

examplesJson.input: db.orders.aggregate([{ $match: { status: { $ne: "cancelled" } } }, { $group: { _id: { $dateToString: { format: "%Y-%m", date: "$placedAt" } }, orders: { $sum: 1 }, revenue: { $sum: "$total" } } }, { $sort: { _id: 1 } }])
examplesJson.output: [ { _id: '2024-01', orders: 2, revenue: 240 }, { _id: '2024-02', orders: 2, revenue: 150 }, { _id: '2024-03', orders: 3, revenue: 300 }, { _id: '2024-04', orders: 3, revenue: 280 }, { _id: '2024-05', orders: 2, revenue: 440 }, { _id: '2024-06', orders: 3, revenue: 220 }, { _id: '2024-07', orders: 3, revenue: 320 } ]
examplesJson.explanation: February shows 2 orders and 150 because the 300 cancelled order 5 was filtered out; May shows 440 for the same reason (order 13 removed).

examplesJson.input: db.orders.aggregate([{ $match: { status: { $ne: "cancelled" } } }, { $group: { _id: { $dateTrunc: { date: "$placedAt", unit: "quarter" } }, revenue: { $sum: "$total" } } }, { $sort: { _id: 1 } }])
examplesJson.explanation: Q1 is 240 + 150 + 300 = 690 and Q2 is 280 + 440 + 220 = 940, matching the monthly numbers. $dateTrunc keeps the key a Date pinned to the first instant of the quarter.

hintsJson: The period is not stored anywhere — you have to compute it from placedAt inside the group key.
A $group _id can be any expression, so put the date operator directly in it.
$dateToString: { format: "%Y-%m", date: "$placedAt" } gives a sortable string; %m is zero padded.
$dateTrunc: { date: "$placedAt", unit: "quarter" } rounds down to 2024-01-01, 2024-04-01 or 2024-07-01 and stays a Date.

starterCodeJson.name: pipeline.js

solutionCodeJson.name: pipeline.js

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The group key is an expression, so the time bucket is computed on the fly and never has to be stored. $dateToString with format: "%Y-%m" turns every placedAt into a four-digit year, a hyphen and a zero-padded month, so January 2024 becomes "2024-01". Zero padding is what makes the trailing $sort: { _id: 1 } chronological: string comparison puts "2024-10" after "2024-09", whereas an unpadded "2024-9" would sort after "2024-10" and silently scramble a year-long report.

$dateTrunc takes the other approach: it rounds the date down to the start of the requested unit and returns a Date. Every Q2 order collapses to 2024-04-01T00:00:00Z, which sorts correctly by nature, can be compared with range operators, and can be formatted for display later. Prefer it when the bucket key will be used in further date arithmetic, and prefer $dateToString when the key is a label you are about to render. The cross-check built into this exercise — 690 + 940 + 320 equals the seven monthly figures summed — is the habit worth keeping: two aggregations over the same filtered set at different granularities must reconcile, and a mismatch always means the filters diverged.

The production trap is time zones. Both operators default to UTC, so an order placed at 23:30 local time in Hanoi lands in the previous UTC day and can be counted in the wrong month at a boundary. Both accept a timezone option — { format: "%Y-%m", date: "$placedAt", timezone: "Asia/Ho_Chi_Minh" } or the same key on $dateTrunc — and stating it explicitly is the only way a daily or monthly report agrees with what the business sees. A second, quieter gap: months with no orders produce no group at all, because aggregation can only summarise documents that exist. Dense time series with zero-filled gaps require joining against a generated calendar, not a bigger pipeline.

exercises.title: Join Orders to Customers with $lookup in Both Forms
exercises.problemHtml:
$lookup is MongoDB's left outer join. It runs against another collection in the same database and attaches the matching documents as an array field on each input document — always an array, even when exactly one document matches, and an empty array when none do. Two forms exist: the equality form with localField/foreignField, which is the fast path for a simple foreign-key match, and the pipeline form with let and an inner pipeline, which lets you add conditions, project, sort or limit the joined side.

The customers collection holds c1 to c5 with a name and tier. Write two pipelines:

  • Equality form: match orders 1 and 2, $lookup from customers on localField: "customerId" and foreignField: "_id" into as: "customer", $unwind that array to flatten the single match, then $project total plus name and tier lifted from "$customer.name" and "$customer.tier", sorted by _id.
  • Pipeline form, starting from customers: for each customer, join their bigOrders — orders where customerId equals the outer _id, total is at least 150, and status is not cancelled. Bind the outer id with let: { cid: "$_id" }, compare it inside the inner $match using $expr, project only total on the joined side and sort it by _id. Then $project name and bigOrders and sort by _id.

Watch what happens to the customer with no qualifying orders — the join keeps the row and gives it an empty array. The scaffold provides both skeletons.
exercises.inputSpec: An orders collection of 20 documents with fields _id (1..20), customerId, region (north/south/east/west), status (shipped/pending/delivered/cancelled), total, placedAt (dates through 2024), an items array of { sku, qty, price } sub-documents, and an optional coupon field. Order 16 has an empty items array and order 18 has no items field. A customers collection holds c1..c5 with name, tier and city.
exercises.outputSpec: The equality-form pipeline returns [ { _id: 1, total: 90, name: 'Ana Vu', tier: 'gold' }, { _id: 2, total: 150, name: 'Bao Le', tier: 'silver' } ]. The pipeline form returns five customers: c1 with orders 14 and 20, c2 with orders 2 and 17, c3 with order 8, c4 with an empty bigOrders array, and c5 with order 11.
exercises.constraints: Both collections live in the same database — $lookup cannot cross databases. Inside the pipeline form you must use $expr to compare a field against a let variable; a plain equality match on "$$cid" will not work. Do not simulate the join with two round trips.

examplesJson.input: db.orders.aggregate([{ $match: { _id: { $in: [1, 2] } } }, { $lookup: { from: "customers", localField: "customerId", foreignField: "_id", as: "customer" } }, { $unwind: "$customer" }, { $project: { total: 1, name: "$customer.name", tier: "$customer.tier" } }, { $sort: { _id: 1 } }])
examplesJson.output: [ { _id: 1, total: 90, name: 'Ana Vu', tier: 'gold' }, { _id: 2, total: 150, name: 'Bao Le', tier: 'silver' } ]
examplesJson.explanation: Each order matches exactly one customer, so the joined array has one element; $unwind flattens it and the projection lifts the two fields to the top level.

examplesJson.input: db.customers.aggregate([{ $lookup: { from: "orders", let: { cid: "$_id" }, pipeline: [{ $match: { $expr: { $and: [{ $eq: ["$customerId", "$$cid"] }, { $gte: ["$total", 150] }, { $ne: ["$status", "cancelled"] }] } } }, { $project: { total: 1 } }, { $sort: { _id: 1 } }], as: "bigOrders" } }, { $project: { name: 1, bigOrders: 1 } }, { $sort: { _id: 1 } }])
examplesJson.output: [ { _id: 'c1', name: 'Ana Vu', bigOrders: [ { _id: 14, total: 320 }, { _id: 20, total: 220 } ] }, { _id: 'c2', name: 'Bao Le', bigOrders: [ { _id: 2, total: 150 }, { _id: 17, total: 150 } ] }, { _id: 'c3', name: 'Chi Tran', bigOrders: [ { _id: 8, total: 160 } ] }, { _id: 'c4', name: 'Dat Pham', bigOrders: [] }, { _id: 'c5', name: 'En Ngo', bigOrders: [ { _id: 11, total: 170 } ] } ]
examplesJson.explanation: Dat Pham's only order of 150 or more is the cancelled order 5, so the inner pipeline rejects it and the join returns an empty array rather than dropping the customer.

hintsJson: A join here means attaching documents from another collection as an array field.
The simple form needs four keys: from, localField, foreignField and as.
When exactly one document can match, $unwind the joined array immediately so later stages see a plain sub-document.
The pipeline form binds outer values with let: { cid: "$_id" } and reads them as "$$cid" inside an $expr comparison such as { $eq: ["$customerId", "$$cid"] }.

starterCodeJson.name: pipeline.js

solutionCodeJson.name: pipeline.js

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The equality form is a foreign-key join and nothing more: for each incoming order, find every customers document whose _id equals this order's customerId, and put the results in customer. The result is always an array — that is the part people forget, and it is why "$customer.name" on the un-unwound array would give you [ 'Ana Vu' ] rather than a string. Since the relationship is many-to-one, $unwind immediately after the lookup flattens the one-element array into a sub-document and the projection can then lift fields out of it. If a match were ever missing, that $unwind would silently drop the order, so add preserveNullAndEmptyArrays: true whenever the foreign document is not guaranteed to exist.

The pipeline form exists because the equality form can only test equality. Here the join must also require total of at least 150 and a non-cancelled status, so the outer document's _id is bound to a variable with let: { cid: "$_id" } and read inside the inner pipeline as "$$cid". The double dollar is significant: $field means "a field of the document currently being examined" (a document from orders, the joined collection) while $$variable means "a variable from the enclosing scope". Because the comparison mixes a field with a variable, it must live inside $expr, which lets aggregation expressions run in a query context; writing { customerId: "$$cid" } without $expr would search for the literal string.

Note that $lookup is a left outer join in both forms: Dat Pham's only order of 150 or more is cancelled, so his bigOrders is [] and his row survives — you would filter those out yourself with a following $match on the array size if you wanted an inner join. Performance-wise, the join executes the inner lookup once per input document, so an index on the joined field (orders.customerId here) is what keeps it from degrading into a collection scan per row; that is why joining after a $match that shrinks the driving side, as the first pipeline does, is usually much faster than joining everything and filtering later. Also remember the joined collection must be in the same database and, before MongoDB 5.1, could not be sharded.

exercises.title: Build a Multi-Branch Dashboard with $facet, $bucket and $count
exercises.problemHtml:
A dashboard usually needs several different summaries of the same documents: a histogram, a breakdown by status, and a headline total. Running three pipelines means reading the collection three times. $facet solves this by taking a set of named sub-pipelines, feeding each one the identical input stream, and emitting a single document whose fields are the sub-pipelines' results as arrays. $bucket complements it by producing a histogram: you supply sorted boundaries and it assigns each document to the bucket whose lower bound it falls in, half-open — a value equal to a boundary goes into the bucket that starts there.

Produce a one-document dashboard over all 20 orders in orders, with three facets:

  • byTotalBucket: a $bucket on groupBy: "$total" with boundaries: [0, 50, 100, 200, 400], default: "other", and an output of count as { $sum: 1 } and revenue as { $sum: "$total" }.
  • byStatus: a $group on "$status" counting into n, sorted by _id ascending.
  • total: a single { $count: "orders" }.

Two details to get right. The bucket _id in the output is the lower boundary of the bucket, not a range object, and every document must fall inside boundaries or into the default bucket — without a default, an out-of-range value aborts the pipeline. And because $facet results are arrays inside one document, each facet needs its own $sort; sorting outside the facet is meaningless. The scaffold provides the stage skeleton.
exercises.inputSpec: An orders collection of 20 documents with fields _id (1..20), customerId, region (north/south/east/west), status (shipped/pending/delivered/cancelled), total, placedAt (dates through 2024), an items array of { sku, qty, price } sub-documents, and an optional coupon field. Order 16 has an empty items array and order 18 has no items field. A customers collection holds c1..c5 with name, tier and city. All 20 orders are included, including the two cancelled ones. Totals range from 0 to 320.
exercises.outputSpec: One document. byTotalBucket is [ { _id: 0, count: 4, revenue: 50 }, { _id: 50, count: 5, revenue: 320 }, { _id: 100, count: 8, revenue: 1090 }, { _id: 200, count: 3, revenue: 840 } ]; byStatus is [ { _id: 'cancelled', n: 2 }, { _id: 'delivered', n: 7 }, { _id: 'pending', n: 3 }, { _id: 'shipped', n: 8 } ]; total is [ { orders: 20 } ].
exercises.constraints: One aggregate call only — do not run three separate pipelines. boundaries must be sorted ascending and of a single type. Supply a default bucket. Sort inside each facet, never after $facet.

examplesJson.input: The byTotalBucket facet
examplesJson.output: [ { _id: 0, count: 4, revenue: 50 }, { _id: 50, count: 5, revenue: 320 }, { _id: 100, count: 8, revenue: 1090 }, { _id: 200, count: 3, revenue: 840 } ]
examplesJson.explanation: Bucket 0 holds totals in [0, 50): the two zero-total orders plus 20 and 30, so revenue 50. Bucket 100 holds [100, 200) — eight orders summing to 1090. The counts add to 20 and the revenues to 2300, the collection total.

examplesJson.input: The byStatus and total facets
examplesJson.output: byStatus: [ { _id: 'cancelled', n: 2 }, { _id: 'delivered', n: 7 }, { _id: 'pending', n: 3 }, { _id: 'shipped', n: 8 } ]; total: [ { orders: 20 } ]
examplesJson.explanation: Both facets see the same 20 input documents as the bucket facet. 2 + 7 + 3 + 8 = 20, which is the cross-check that the facets partition one stream.

examplesJson.input: db.orders.aggregate([{ $facet: { total: [{ $count: "orders" }] } }])
examplesJson.output: [ { total: [ { orders: 20 } ] } ]
examplesJson.explanation: Even a single-document facet result is wrapped in an array, so reading it in application code means result[0].total[0].orders.

hintsJson: Three summaries of one stream in one pass means one stage that fans the stream out.
$facet takes an object of name -> sub-pipeline array; each sub-pipeline receives the identical input documents.
$bucket needs groupBy, boundaries, default and output; boundaries are half-open so [0, 50) excludes 50.
The bucket output _id is the lower boundary; put each facet’s $sort inside that facet, and remember every facet value comes back as an array.

starterCodeJson.name: pipeline.js

solutionCodeJson.name: pipeline.js

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
$facet is a fan-out: the documents that reach it are handed, unchanged, to every named sub-pipeline, and the stage emits exactly one document whose fields are those sub-pipelines' outputs. Three summaries therefore cost one pass over the collection instead of three, and — more importantly for correctness — all three are guaranteed to describe the same snapshot of data. That is why the numbers reconcile: the bucket counts sum to 20, the status counts sum to 20, and the total facet reports 20.

$bucket is a histogram with boundaries you choose. Each document is placed in the bucket whose range contains its groupBy value, and the ranges are half-open: [0, 50) includes 0 and excludes 50, so the order totalling exactly 50 lands in the _id: 50 bucket rather than the _id: 0 one. The bucket's _id in the output is its lower boundary — a naked number, not a range object — so a readable report usually adds an $addFields with a label. The default key is not optional in practice: any document whose value falls outside the outermost boundaries, or is of a different type, raises an error unless default catches it. Here no total exceeds 400, so the "other" bucket emits no document at all, which is itself a useful signal that the boundaries cover the data. When you do not know the distribution in advance, $bucketAuto picks boundaries for you to produce roughly equal-sized buckets.

Two practical traps. First, every facet value is an array even when it holds one document, so application code reads result[0].total[0].orders — nesting that surprises people who expected a scalar. Second, sorting must happen inside a facet; once $facet has emitted its single document there is nothing left to sort, so a $sort after it does nothing. Be aware too that each sub-pipeline's output is subject to the 16 MB document limit because everything lands in one document, which is why facets should end in aggregates or bounded $limit lists rather than dumping whole collections, and that $facet cannot contain $out, $merge or another $facet.

exercises.title: Materialise a Product Sales Report with $unwind, $group and $merge
exercises.problemHtml:
Expensive reports should not be recomputed on every page load. The aggregation framework can write its own output back into a collection, turning a pipeline into a materialised view that a plain find can serve in milliseconds. $out replaces a target collection wholesale; $merge upserts document by document on a key, so it can update an existing report incrementally and leave unrelated rows in place. Both must be the last stage of the pipeline.

Build a per-SKU sales report from the orders collection. The sku_summary collection has been pre-seeded with one stale row { _id: "XX", unitsSold: 0, revenue: 0, orderCount: 0 } for a SKU that no longer sells:

  • $match out cancelled orders, then $unwind the items array so each line item is its own document.
  • $group by "$items.sku", computing unitsSold as { $sum: "$items.qty" }, revenue as { $sum: { $multiply: ["$items.qty", "$items.price"] } }, and orderIds as { $addToSet: "$_id" }.
  • $addFields an orderCount of { $size: "$orderIds" }, then $project with orderIds: 0 to drop the helper array.
  • End with { $merge: { into: "sku_summary", whenMatched: "replace", whenNotMatched: "insert" } }, then read the report back with db.sku_summary.find().sort({ revenue: -1 }).toArray().
  • Run the same pipeline again but ending in { $out: "sku_snapshot" }, and read that collection back sorted the same way. Compare the two collections' document counts and explain the difference.

Note that the $group key becomes the _id of every written document, which is what gives $merge something to match on. The scaffold provides the pipeline skeleton.
exercises.inputSpec: An orders collection of 20 documents with fields _id (1..20), customerId, region (north/south/east/west), status (shipped/pending/delivered/cancelled), total, placedAt (dates through 2024), an items array of { sku, qty, price } sub-documents, and an optional coupon field. Order 16 has an empty items array and order 18 has no items field. A customers collection holds c1..c5 with name, tier and city. The sku_summary collection already contains one stale document { _id: "XX", unitsSold: 0, revenue: 0, orderCount: 0 }. sku_snapshot does not exist yet.
exercises.outputSpec: After the $merge, sku_summary holds 6 documents: the 5 computed rows MN (unitsSold 6, revenue 900, orderCount 5), KB (8, 400, 5), PD (8, 240, 4), CB (21, 210, 5), MS (10, 200, 6), plus the untouched stale row XX with revenue 0. After the $out, sku_snapshot holds exactly those 5 computed rows and no XX row.
exercises.constraints: $merge and $out must be the final stage. Group on "$items.sku" so the SKU becomes the written _id. Do not write the report with a client-side loop of insert calls. Read the results back with find().sort(), since neither $out nor $merge returns documents.

examplesJson.input: db.sku_summary.find().sort({ revenue: -1 }).toArray()  (after the $merge)
examplesJson.output: [ { _id: 'MN', unitsSold: 6, revenue: 900, orderCount: 5 }, { _id: 'KB', unitsSold: 8, revenue: 400, orderCount: 5 }, { _id: 'PD', unitsSold: 8, revenue: 240, orderCount: 4 }, { _id: 'CB', unitsSold: 21, revenue: 210, orderCount: 5 }, { _id: 'MS', unitsSold: 10, revenue: 200, orderCount: 6 }, { _id: 'XX', unitsSold: 0, revenue: 0, orderCount: 0 } ]
examplesJson.explanation: Five SKUs were upserted by _id and the pre-existing XX row survived, because $merge only touches documents whose _id the pipeline produced. Revenues sum to 1950, the value of all non-cancelled orders.

examplesJson.input: db.sku_snapshot.find().sort({ revenue: -1 }).toArray()  (after the same pipeline ending in $out)
examplesJson.output: [ { _id: 'MN', unitsSold: 6, revenue: 900, orderCount: 5 }, { _id: 'KB', unitsSold: 8, revenue: 400, orderCount: 5 }, { _id: 'PD', unitsSold: 8, revenue: 240, orderCount: 4 }, { _id: 'CB', unitsSold: 21, revenue: 210, orderCount: 5 }, { _id: 'MS', unitsSold: 10, revenue: 200, orderCount: 6 } ]
examplesJson.explanation: $out replaced the whole target collection, so only the 5 computed rows exist — no stale XX row. That is the difference between replace-everything and upsert-by-key.

examplesJson.input: The CB row, traced by hand
examplesJson.output: { _id: 'CB', unitsSold: 21, revenue: 210, orderCount: 5 }
examplesJson.explanation: CB appears in orders 4 (qty 3), 8 (1), 9 (5), 14 (2) and 19 (10) — 21 units at 10 each is 210, across 5 distinct orders. Cancelled order 13 contributes nothing because $match removed it before the $unwind.

hintsJson: Per-SKU numbers need the line items as separate documents first, then a regrouping on the SKU.
After $unwind, each document has one element at "$items", so group with _id: "$items.sku" and sum "$items.qty".
Line revenue is qty times price: { $sum: { $multiply: ["$items.qty", "$items.price"] } }. Count distinct orders with $addToSet then $size.
{ $merge: { into: "sku_summary", whenMatched: "replace", whenNotMatched: "insert" } } must be last; $out: "sku_snapshot" replaces its target entirely.

starterCodeJson.name: pipeline.js

solutionCodeJson.name: pipeline.js

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
This is the whole module in one pipeline. $match comes first so the two cancelled orders never reach the expensive stages — filtering after the $unwind would produce the same answer but multiply documents that are about to be thrown away. $unwind then converts 18 order documents into 25 line-item documents, each carrying the parent order's _id alongside a single items sub-document. That duplication is what makes the regrouping possible: $group on "$items.sku" re-partitions the stream by product instead of by order, and { $multiply: ["$items.qty", "$items.price"] } computes each line's revenue before $sum folds it in. The totals reconcile exactly — 900 + 400 + 240 + 210 + 200 = 1950, the sum of all non-cancelled order totals.

Counting distinct orders per SKU needs care after an unwind, because { $sum: 1 } would count line items, not orders. Collecting { $addToSet: "$_id" } and taking its $size gives the distinct-order count instead: MS appears on 6 different orders while CB, despite selling 21 units, appears on only 5. The helper array is then dropped with { $project: { orderIds: 0 } } — an exclusion projection, the one case where you name what to remove rather than what to keep.

The two write stages differ in exactly the way the results show. $out is replace-the-collection: it writes to a temporary collection and atomically swaps it in, so sku_snapshot ends up holding precisely the five computed documents and nothing else, and any index or stale row that was there before is gone. $merge is upsert-by-key: for each output document it looks for a target document matching _id (the default on field, which is why grouping by SKU matters) and applies whenMatched, inserting when nothing matches. The stale XX row therefore survives untouched — $merge never deletes. That property is what makes it the tool for incremental rollups: re-run the pipeline over just the last hour's orders with whenMatched: "merge" and you update only the affected SKUs instead of rebuilding the whole report. It also means a shrinking dataset can leave phantom rows behind, so a periodic full rebuild with $out, or an explicit cleanup, is part of the design. Both stages must be last, neither returns documents to the caller, and $out cannot target the collection being read while $merge can.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[orders 20 docs] --> B[match not cancelled]
  B --> C[18 docs]
  C --> D[unwind items]
  D --> E[25 line item docs]
  E --> F[group by sku sum qty and revenue]
  F --> G[5 sku docs]
  G --> H[addFields orderCount then project away helper]
  H --> I[merge into sku summary upsert by id]
  H --> J[out to sku snapshot replaces collection]
